title: Why the return of presence in your own life is the ultimate act of recovery
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

htmlContent:
You left your body a long time ago. It kept you alive.

I remember the exact moment I realized I wasn't living in my own life anymore. I was standing at a kitchen counter, staring at a coffee mug, and I felt nothing. Not tired. Not sad. Just empty. Like I was watching a movie of myself from three feet behind my own eyes. That distance, that hollow feeling, that's what dissociation feels like. And here's the thing: I didn't even know I had left.

Your nervous system did something brilliant when you were young. It decided that staying present in your body was too painful. Too dangerous. So it pulled you out. It created a buffer between you and your own experience. Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, calls this the body's way of surviving the unsurvivable. And he's right. Your system chose protection over presence because presence felt like death.

But here's what nobody tells you: that survival strategy eventually becomes the cage you can't escape. You stop feeling your own hunger. You stop noticing when you're cold. You stop knowing what you want. You become a ghost in your own skin. And the worst part? You don't even realize you're haunting yourself.

I spent years thinking I was just "calm under pressure." That I was "good in a crisis." That I didn't get rattled like other people. What I actually was, was disconnected. I had learned to leave my body so completely that I could watch my own life fall apart without flinching. That's not strength. That's survival. And survival is not the same as living.

So when I say the return of presence is the ultimate act of recovery, I mean it literally. You have to come back. You have to reclaim the territory of your own body. You have to learn to feel again, even when feeling hurts. Especially when it hurts.

The nervous system doesn't forget. It just waits.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades showing us something terrifying and beautiful: your body remembers everything your mind tried to forget. Every moment you checked out. Every time you numbed. Every time you told yourself "it wasn't that bad." Your nervous system catalogued it all. And it's been waiting for you to come back.

Look, I know this is heavy material. And I know there's a part of you that might be thinking "this is too much" or "I can't handle this" or "maybe I should just close this tab." That part is trying to protect you. And that's okay. But I want you to know that you can handle this. You've already handled worse. The fact that you're here, reading this, means your survival system did its job. You made it.

What we're doing here isn't about reliving the past. It's about understanding it well enough to stop being controlled by it. There's a difference between being trapped in a memory and looking at it from a safe distance. You get to choose how close you get. You get to choose when to step back. That choice - that agency - is itself a form of healing.

Think about it. When was the last time you actually felt your feet on the floor? Not thought about your feet. Actually felt them. The pressure. The texture of your socks. The temperature of the ground beneath you. If you're like I was, that question feels almost absurd. Why would I do that? What's the point?

The point is that your body is the only place you can actually heal. You can't think your way out of trauma. You can't talk your way out of a dysregulated nervous system. You can't positive-affirmation your way back into your own skin. Recovery happens in the body, through the body, with the body. And that means you have to be present to do it.

I know how hard this is. I know because I've sat in my own therapy sessions, shaking, sweating, wanting to run, wanting to disappear, wanting to do anything except stay in my body. The urge to leave is so strong. It's been your survival instinct for years. Decades maybe. And now someone is asking you to do the exact opposite of what kept you safe. Stay with me here. I know it sounds impossible.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She's a clinician who studies the polyvagal theory Stephen Porges developed. And what she offers is not theory. It's practice. It's small, daily ways to start noticing your nervous system without trying to fix it. Without trying to force it to be different. Just noticing. That's where presence begins. In the noticing.

The wellness industry sold you a lie about presence

Let me be direct about something. The wellness industry has turned "presence" into a product. They want you to think it's about candles and meditation apps and perfectly morning routines. They want you to believe that if you just try harder, breathe deeper, buy the right crystals, you'll finally feel whole. That's not presence. That's performance. And your nervous system knows the difference.

Real presence is not pretty. It's not Instagrammable. It's sitting in your car after a therapy session, sobbing so hard you can't see the steering wheel. It's feeling the rage you've been swallowing for thirty years. It's noticing the shame that lives in your chest like a second heartbeat. It's staying when every fiber of your being screams at you to run.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. She writes about something called the "trance of unworthiness." That trance is what happens when you've been absent from yourself so long that you believe you're broken. Radical acceptance is the practice of coming back. Of saying "I'm here, and I'm okay, even if it doesn't feel like it."

I want you to notice something. Right now. In this moment. What's happening in your body as you read these words? Are you holding your breath? Are your shoulders tight? Is there a hollow feeling in your stomach? That's your nervous system responding. That's the beginning of presence. Not doing something. Not fixing something. Just noticing.

Sit with that for a second. Don't try to change it. Don't try to breathe it away. Don't tell yourself a story about what it means. Just feel it. That's it. That's the whole practice. Coming back, over and over, to what's actually here.

The return is not a destination. It's a practice.

Here's the part nobody wants to tell you. You will leave your body again. You will dissociate again. You will check out when things get hard. That's not failure. That's your nervous system doing its job. The goal is not to never leave. The goal is to notice when you've left, and to find your way back. Over and over. For the rest of your life.

I still lose myself. I still find myself staring at walls, disconnected, hollow. The difference now is that I know what's happening. I can feel the absence. And I have tools to return. Sometimes it takes five minutes. Sometimes it takes days. But I always come back now. That's the recovery. Not perfection. Return.

Let me be honest with you about something. Recovery is expensive. Not just financially - though therapy and bodywork and supplements and books add up fast. It's expensive in time, in energy, in relationships. Some people won't understand why you're changing. Some people benefited from your old patterns and they won't like the new ones. That's real. And it's painful.

But here's what's also real. The cost of not healing is higher. It's paid in health - in autoimmune conditions, in chronic pain, in shortened lifespan. It's paid in relationships - in repeating the same patterns, in choosing the same unavailable partners, in pushing away the people who could actually love you. It's paid in aliveness - in years spent numb, disconnected, going through the motions.

I'm not saying this to scare you. I'm saying it because the choice to heal isn't a luxury. It's a necessity. And it's one that only you can make. No one can do this work for you. But you don't have to do it alone. That's the paradox of recovery - it's deeply personal work that requires other people. You need witnesses. You need co-regulators. You need at least one person who can see you clearly and not look away. Find that person. Or let them find you.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey. Perry is a neuroscientist who studies how trauma shapes the developing brain. The question "what happened to you?" instead of "what's wrong with you?" changes everything. It opens the door to presence because it stops the shame spiral. It lets you see your disconnection as adaptation, not defect.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay Gibson. If you grew up having to manage your parents' emotions, if you learned to disappear to keep the peace, this book will name something you've felt your whole life. That survival strategy of leaving your body? It made sense. It was necessary. But you don't need it anymore.

I'm not going to tell you this is easy. It's not. Coming back to your body after years of absence is like learning to walk after a long illness. You will stumble. You will fall. You will want to give up. But here's what I know: every time you come back, you build a little more trust with yourself. Every time you stay present through discomfort, your nervous system learns that it's safe to feel. Not safe because the world changed. Safe because you showed up for yourself.

The body is not your enemy. It's your home.

I used to hate my body. Not because of how it looked, but because of what it held. The memories. The sensations. The unprocessed grief. I wanted to escape it constantly. And I did. For years. But here's the thing I learned in my own recovery: the body is not the source of the pain. It's the container for it. And if you never come back to the container, you can never let the pain move through you.

Janina Fisher, a trauma therapist who works with structural dissociation, says that the body is not the problem. The body is the solution. Your nervous system holds the blueprint for your healing. It knows what it needs. It knows how to discharge the stuck energy. It knows how to complete the survival responses that got interrupted. But you have to be present to let it happen.

So what does this return actually look like? It looks like stopping mid-day and putting your hand on your chest. It looks like feeling the weight of your body in your chair. It looks like noticing the temperature of the air on your skin. It looks like saying "I'm here" out loud, even if your voice shakes. It looks like staying when you want to run.

It looks like failure. And trying again. And failure. And trying again. Until the trying becomes easier than the running.

I'm not going to wrap this up neatly. I'm not going to tell you that if you just do these things, you'll be healed. Because that's not how this works. Recovery is not linear. It's not a before-and-after. It's a constant, messy, exhausting, beautiful return to yourself. Over and over. Until the return becomes your new normal.

But I will tell you this: you are worth coming back for. The life you're living from three feet behind your eyes is not the only life available to you. There is a version of you that feels the sun on their skin. That tastes their food. That cries when they're sad and laughs when they're happy. That version is not gone. They're just waiting for you to come home.

So come home. One breath at a time. One sensation at a time. One moment of presence at a time. Your body has been waiting for you. It's time to return.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - A book that many find validates their childhood experience
